speaker: interviewer
text: What year were you conscripted into the Polish army?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1938, February the 16th-- I remember that day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do while you were in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the beginning, naturally, we was training, and training [? all ?] kind of like any soldier. And then, like my profession is a, a blacksmith. And they need a blacksmith. They took me as a, as a blacksmith.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you work as a blacksmith?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the blacksmith shop, in the army blacksmith shop. And I wasn't-- there was two kind of blacksmiths around there. Was blacksmith what they used to shoe horses and blacksmiths what they used to do all kinds of work what they needed-- no horses. And that-- I was doing that kind of work, wagon repairs, person, all kind of whatever, anything what they need to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many hours a day would you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Depend-- mostly all day, [? about ?] eight hours, seven hours, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened on the high holidays or on Shabbos? Were you given any time off?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbos, no-- we, we used to work. Shabbos wasn't any holiday. Now when was a holiday, like a big holiday, like Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, or Shavuot, or Pesach, they used take us, the Jewish soldiers, to a, to a synagogue, and then, like Pesach, to a kosher kitchen. They used to give us meals.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the kosher kitchen located?

speaker: interviewee
text: The kosher kitchen was located close to [? Eishyshok, ?] like in [? Villna. ?] And we used to hear pretty good speakers and very good [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the speakers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Mostly, one time was speaking a Jewish captain, a Jewish captain from the, from the army. He was speaking in Polish. And he said, on our shoulders is all the Judaism exist. Because some people was complaining they were taking the Jewish people in the army, the discrimination, everything. And when he brought it out. He said openly, exactly, or [? we were ?] supposed to glad they are take us to the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did he give that speech?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think on [? Shavuot. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I supposed to be discharged in a couple weeks. And then the war started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the war, I was in-- they took us to the front. Before that, I remember, it was a holiday, a military holiday from a regiment, [INAUDIBLE] some kind of holiday. And there supposed to came a speaker to speak, was came then after-- supposed to came that, supposed to came the president from Poland that time. Well, he don't came. Came Rydz-Smigly. He was the, the head of the military. And he was having a speech. And then the soldiers, the Polish soldiers, they start to yell. They want to go on Berlin-- that time was already [INAUDIBLE]-- and Berlin, and Berlin, and Berlin. I think to myself that they was yelling at Berlin. They don't know what they're talking.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, shortly, there was the alarm to go to the front. And that same fellow, what he was yelling on Berlin, start to cry. I said, what are you crying for? You supposed to be happy. You're going-- you yell not [? where ?] I [? want ?] go so loud. You want to go on Berlin. Now you have a chance to go to kill the Germans. Ah, he said, I'm married and this. I said, so what? You were yelling Berlin. Be glad you got it. That's what you want. Finally, he stopped. He don't talk anymore. Where on the front where you sent? The front was--

speaker: interviewee
text: We went close to the German border. And down there, we were standing from the beginning. Then we start to fire. And mostly, we'd fire on old cities to be-- why they fire on the old cities? They said because they couldn't see the good target. They fire special, not by accident. And secondly, before we came to the front, they told us all kind of rumors when we was going by train to the front. The our Polish soldiers knocked out-- knock down so many things, knocked out. And so many place, they shut down, and so many prisoners. We thought that's true. And then when we came by the, by train in our town, we unload our artillery. And I saw a little market down there. And I see people crying and got some animals dead, pigs, and geese, and ducks, and chickens. And I don't know what it is. I-- and that officer said not to go on over.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I went down there, and I, and I see a woman is crying, I ask her, what, what are you crying for? And she told me, said, mister, go ahead. Do something. The, the Germans come in in our place. And they taken away our men. They taken away everything. They beating us and everything. Now I find out the truth, you know? Then, when I come back and that officer start to yell at me, where you been? I said, I just went for a walk. I  told you not to go nowhere. I said, I haven't been nowhere. And then I find out it's not true, everything what they are telling us. Had you ever heard of Adolf Hitler at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, we heard of Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know about Hitler and the Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: We know for the Jewish of Hitler, it will succeeded, it would be very bad. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: We know exactly everything. Did you-- how long did you fight on the front?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very long-- we, a few weeks. [INAUDIBLE] And then, and then was all kind of rumors. They said Hitler got hanged, this and this. And the war, the war is over. We was going to the war. The war was over. Finally, we came to our town. And they, they start to-- them soldiers, what they run away from the war [INAUDIBLE], they all were beat up to organize them and send them to, on the, for the, the defense on Warsaw. I hear that. I don't like that. I don't show up to that organization. Maybe somebody was going that's voluntarily. I don't show up. I don't wanted to be registered and to go on defense on Warsaw. Because I know that's the end. It doesn't matter what kind of defense they are going to put together, it wouldn't stay too long.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you decide to do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I decide to do-- I came in the town. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which town was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Rovno, ?] then the Russian already. And then we was waiting to see the Russians. Then came one Russian soldier. When he said-- by ourselves together, all the soldiers-- it was a lot of soldiers, Polish soldiers-- and he said, you're all free. You, you can go home.

speaker: interviewee
text: That time was the season for potatoes. Said to dig the potatoes and everything, you know? And who was close went home by foot. Or it doesn't matter how he, he can get. And I went and asked for a train to go to [? Vilna. ?] How far was [? Rovno ?] from [? Vilna? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: A few hundred kilometers, naturally-- and they promised us to give us a train. Just got, we've got to wait. By that time, we don't have any food. We start to look for food. I was with a few guys together. And we find out little by little trains for the-- was going for the Polish army with any kind of foo, with the best food. We find bread. We find canned meat and coffee and almond nuts and liquor. Find-- we find everything. Finally, I brought lots of, lots of stuff. And, and some soldiers don't have nothing to eat, was going hungry. I brought maybe a quarter of a bag of cans and lots of bread. We used to cut half a lo-- half a loaf of bread and half a can of meat and give it to the soldiers, because we have too much. Finally, they give us the train. And then we went on the train. It was-- before the train, they line up all the soldiers before the train. And then they said-- they took the, all the, the officers who was able to give the command. And they, they line it up. And then they said the Polish to step out. And the Polish people, Polish soldier by their nationality, they step out. Then Ukrainians step out. And the Ukrainians step out. Then Belarus, the White Russians, step out. It was a-- then I heard said, White Russian to join the Ukrainian. And the Polish, they took it away. I don't know where.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then were left over a few Jewish people and Muslims, Tatars-- same thing. Come, they ask us, who are you? We said, we are Jewish. And who was them? Tatars.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, they said, Jewish to the White Russian and the Ukrainian. We joined them. Then they give us the train. And they said us, they, they're going to give the train to Vilna. Now, they don't took us to Vilna. They took the train to Russia or just wasn't [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked down, we and another fellow, to buy some bread, to get some bread. And the train left. And that's why we are left. And that fellow said to me, whose-- well, how are we going to go with it? Will be another train, I said to him. Finally, we walk by foot.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came in another town, a town called Nowogrodek. And down there was already organized from the Jewish, the Jewish community, a kitchen for the Jewish soldiers. I find out down there. And I come in, I ask. And they give me eat. And then they gave me a place to sleep. I said, I have a friend, not, not, non-Jewish. Said, take your friend, too. They put us in a hotel. On that night, I have a nightmare of the bombing and everything, I start to scream. Well, he took his hand, put on my mouth. And the next day, we went-- we walk again. We walk closer to [? town ?] [INAUDIBLE]. And we came even to [? Istan, ?] and I went to Lida. I went to Lida. Come in Lida, the Russians already there. And then I met, accidentally, your brother. Because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's brother. And they give him-- somebody give him, a friend of his, a, a civilian suit. He changed the clothes to civilian. And then, and then they hire for us-- I don't know who pay for this-- a horse and wagon to take us from Lida to a town, Eisiskes, Eisiskes. Because he got to go-- they took us [INAUDIBLE] from Eishyshok. I comes-- not far from Eishyshok, I went to [? Natchaug ?] to see, to my mother. And he went to Eishyshok [INAUDIBLE], I told them to give to my relatives a regard. When I come to [? Natchaug, ?] and I come in, I'm-- because we were four brothers in the army, in the war. When I come in, in the house, if I would met my mother in the street, I would never recognize her.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had had happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: She so changed-- old, sick. I wouldn't recognize her, that's for sure. And when she asked me-- she saw me, she wasn't so excited, because she was so sick. She asked me about the rest of the boys. I said, they'll come. They'll come. I have no idea where they are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Two days later, my older brother, Yakov come. And a month later, Chaim come. He was in Russia. Yakov, Yakov wasn't in Russia. And [? Yehuda ?] come too, home, to the-- to his family. He was in Russia too, a month.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay at home in your mother's house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my mother's house, I stay for a while. Then I-- then they started to say they were going to give back that Eishyshok to Lithuania. They make a border. I went to Eishyshok. And I stay in Eishyshok.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you want to be in Eishyshok?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I am-- I know I have friends down there in Eishyshok. And I, I used to work in Eishyshok. And in [? Natchaug ?] was very little. And I don't like to stay in [? Natchaug. ?] And I don't like the, the way the, the Russians were doing. And I was not so crazy about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had you seen about the Russians that you didn't like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The system-- not the people, the system. It was, to me, strange, the system. [? LIBBY ASNER: ?] The people, they are very good people, very [INAUDIBLE] people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you went off to Eishyshok. And Eishyshok was-- became Lithuania.

speaker: interviewee
text: Became Lithuania-- and then I have a, a cousin in Lithuania by the name too, [? Abonele ?] Asner. I never saw him. I know you, you got a cousin. Finally, we get in touch. I went to visit him. I went to visit him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he live?

speaker: interviewee
text: With him-- who, the-- my cousin?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- [? LIBBY ASNER: ?] [INAUDIBLE] The name Olava-- and he had a store. He got a pretty good business. He was serving beer and all kind of merchandise.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was standing with him. And he was glad to see me down there. And then one time, I was talking with my cousin's wife. And she was talking, talking. All of a sudden, she jumped in, how's your mother? She never mentioned my mother before. I just look. And I said, say, why? Do you know something about my mother? She said, no. I said, you must know something. She said, no, no. Finally, I put on a little bit pressure.

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw it when she told me, your mother died. And I said to her, you did it the first thing. But you never told me. Say, I'm entitled to be-- [INAUDIBLE] to my-- to be to my mother's funeral. Finally, I went. And I left everything. I went-- well, naturally, it was too late. And I come back. I come back. And I been for quite-- for a while, maybe a few-- maybe about a week. And then I hear four o'clock, quarter to four in the morning, June the 22nd, 1941, planes roaring. And I hear the sound from the planes. It's not like Russia. The Russian have a different sound. It looks to me-- it's familiar plane to me. I come out. And I see planes that are around, flying. And I see that's not Russian. Some-- something wrong. And they are-- they bombing somewhere and everything. Finally, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] we wait for a while. Then we start to bomb. And I see the Russian running with trucks, by foot. And everywhere, they're, they're running all over. Then my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are they running?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're running, they're running-- they, they don't know where to run. They are running away from the, the [INAUDIBLE] of the trucks. [INAUDIBLE], they're running like to Russia. And when I see-- I hear they saying, the Germans got close. Then mine cousin said, we'll go in a village. And we'll stay overnight. We went in the village.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too far away, maybe about 10, 12 kilometer, or something like that, you know-- not too far. We went to the-- by the next day, it was so quiet, we came back. The Germans already in, so no use to go there. Because the Germans is in. Finally-- this was in the morning-- later at, at night, not before noon, before, before four o'clock, it was a sign hanging out by the Lithuanian. They were ready to make a government with police and everything. Jewish not from that area got to leave the area. If not, they will be shot. I went to the mayor to allow me to stay, because the front is going. I, I can't-- I would glad to go. The front is going. The mayor-- I come in. I said like hello in Lithuanian. He answer me and then start to speak to me in Lithuanian what I want. I understood him pretty good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, just I ask him, maybe you know another language to speak-- maybe Russian or German or Polish? He said yes. I told him I'm Jewish and this. I got to leave the town. Because-- you know, and I couldn't leave because the front is going. And he said to me after, for you, is not dif-- what should be different for you, Germans or Russians? I said to me, is no difference. I've worked for the, for the Polish. And I've worked for the Russians. I hope I'll work for the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I see he opens up the drawer from the-- and I see he's grabbing a, a pistol. And I just head right the door. And I run away, run away. I don't know if he want to shoot me or he want to scare me. I couldn't tell that. I just, I know he grabbed a pistol.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the mayor's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't know the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you run?

speaker: interviewee
text: I run back to, to my cousin. And then I went to German officer. And I told him I'm Jewish. And he said, I don't care if you're Jewish or not. And I asked him if this Eishyshok has been taken over by the Germans. And, and he was very nice. He took out the ma. And he was looking. He told me the points where he's sure the Germans took it over. And he don't tell me. He said, I'm not sure what Eishyshok has take-- taken over. Well, anyway, I got to leave. Because if I wait leave till four o'clock, I'll be shot. I took my things, everything what I had. And I mark it out, where is Polish villages. And I go by foot. And that officer told me, the German, not to go with the side roads. I, like a ex-soldier, I know that, too. I don't-- you don't have to tell me. Oh, he give me the right advice, anyway, not to go with the side roads. If you go with the side roads, you get shot, to go behind the German army. And that's what I did it. I go-- was going behind the German army. Well, when I saw where is the village, close, what I go in. I go in. I speak perfect Polish. And they couldn't recognize me as a Jew. They gave me food and shelter. And the next day, I am taking off again. And I was going every day behind the army. And then I came to one little town. It was a Jewish family. And I know the Jewish family. He don't know me. And I don't know him either, just-- they told me where he lives down there. When I met him, he took me in. And he locked me up. And I was sitting by myself. And I was so lonely, to nobody to-- and I don't know anything what's going on. And I was watching through the window. A little boy come out, a Lithuanian, and start to dance a Russian dance and sing Russian, maybe [INAUDIBLE]. The way was [INAUDIBLE] my heart, I laugh, you know. And his mother come in. And she start to take him-- she chase him away, take him-- took him home. After I left from down there, I was going, again, farther. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] After I was going down there farther. And down there was a big airport the Russians was build-- building, a huge air-- a military airport. When the Germans invaded, they took it over, the airport. And when I was close to the airport, Russian planes come over to bomb the airport. And the Germans fleet, the, the fighters, they-- right away, they took, took off. The fighters, the fighters shot down, right away, the three Russian planes. And all of a sudden, here I am down there. They grabbed me [INAUDIBLE]. And then one-- a couple Germans said-- I answer them in Russian. I took out mine passport and everything. And they was looking at passport. And they couldn't see anything. And then, just saw that I'm a Lithuanian citizen. And they said from the beginning, they [INAUDIBLE]. They check me and took me and took me. I thought, they're going to kill me down there. Finally, I had, I had luck. They let me go. They let me go. Then I was going farther. And I come to the town, Orany. And I was so-- was, the heat was so hot weather. [? And I ?] was so dry. And I was afraid to go in, ask for a drink of water.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in Orany? About what time of the year, what, what month?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in June.

speaker: interviewer
text: In June.

speaker: interviewee
text: In June-- by the end of June. I couldn't tell you the day, because I get mixed up already. I don't know which day it was. And then I came to Orany. And I was-- wanted a drink. And I know the Lithuanians not so friendly. And I saw a well with a pole, with a pail. I start to pull out some water from the well. And the pail was burned.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I pull out, the water run out. Then a lady came out and said to me, you want to drink water? I said, yes. She said, come over. And finally, I told-- she told, talked to me in Lithuanian.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I told her maybe she know how to speak Polish or Russian. She said, I can speak anything, [INAUDIBLE] Jewish, Jewish, too. She recognized me right away. I'm Jewish. Well, she took me in. She give me food and, and shelter. And they was talking against the Germans. And I was afraid maybe they are talking just for, for me. Finally, her brother was-- when he was talking against the Germans and everything, and then Germans come over. They ask him for eggs. And says-- they said they don't have any eggs.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, you have chickens, and you don't have any eggs? He said, no. They took a stick, a pole. They killed the chickens. They said, you don't have any eggs, you don't need-- you don't need any chickens.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they, they, they steal everything. They grab, they grab everything. They steal. The Germans, they took away some sheep and anything that they can-- everything that they can own. That man, the lady's brother, was looking on these. Don't like them at all. And I see he's so mad. Finally, the police find out about me in Lithuania. And the police came over me-- to me. And because down there in Orany was a fight between the Germans and the Russians, lots of buildings was burned and bombed. And down there are mostly farming places. Lots of cattle was killed. And they want us to bury the cattle. And they gave me a shovel. And they gave, gave another few people, and to bury, to pull it out the cattle, bring them in the one place, and to bury them. And that lady, the you know, brother's wife, she saw me. And she knows exactly what's going to happen to me. She saw-- she knows if I got to bury the cattle, they bury me together with the cattle. They're going to kill me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I think we'll have to stop now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop here. This is the end of tape number two.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm suffering an allergic rea--
